pages.text: Gyaltsenglossus senis comes from the middle Cambrian, with fossils found in the Burgess Shale, Canada. It is a small hemichordate, growing up to 2 cm. Despite its small size, Gyaltsenglossus has been notable in figuring out the early evolutionary history of the hemichordates, featuring a mixture of pterobranch-like feeding tentacles on the top, and a large enteropneust-like proboscis tipped body. This would not only help to bring the two major hemichordate classes

together, but would also see Gyaltsenglossus phylogentically placed as the first stem-group hemichordate currently known to science. The generic name derives from the forename of Gyaltsen, the name of the discoverer's father; and the Greek word 'glossa', to mean 'tongue'. The specific name derives from the Latin word 'senex', to mean 'old'.
